name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ لَا تُحَرِّمُوا۟ طَيِّبَـٰتِ مَآ أَحَلَّ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ وَلَا تَعْتَدُوٓا۟ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْمُعْتَدِينَ (٨٧)
```

```english
O you who have faith! Do not prohibit the good things that Allah has made lawful to you, and do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not like the transgressors. (87)
```

```arabic
وَكُلُوا۟ مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ ٱللَّهُ حَلَـٰلًا طَيِّبًا ۚ وَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنتُم بِهِۦ مُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٨)
```

```english
Eat the lawful and good things Allah has provided you, and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith. (88)
```

```arabic
لَا يُؤَاخِذُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِٱللَّغْوِ فِىٓ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ وَلَـٰكِن يُؤَاخِذُكُم بِمَا عَقَّدتُّمُ ٱلْأَيْمَـٰنَ ۖ فَكَفَّـٰرَتُهُۥٓ إِطْعَامُ عَشَرَةِ مَسَـٰكِينَ مِنْ أَوْسَطِ مَا تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ ۖ فَمَن لَّمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيَامُ ثَلَـٰثَةِ أَيَّامٍ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ كَفَّـٰرَةُ أَيْمَـٰنِكُمْ إِذَا حَلَفْتُمْ ۚ وَٱحْفَظُوٓا۟ أَيْمَـٰنَكُمْ ۚ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ (٨٩)
```

```english
Allah shall not take you to task for what is frivolous in your oaths; but He shall take you to task for what you pledge in earnest. The atonement for it is to feed ten needy persons with the average food you give to your families, or their clothing, or the freeing of a slave. He who cannot afford [any of these] shall fast for three days. That is the atonement for your oaths when you vow. But keep your oaths. Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may give thanks. (89)
```

## (Commentary)

These three verses, along with several subsequent verses up to verse one hundred and some of the chapter, are verses clarifying several subsidiary rulings (فروع الأحكام). They are all interspersed, as it were, between the verses addressing the stories of the Messiah (عليه‌السلام) and the Christians. Because they consist of scattered groups revealed concerning diverse rulings, each being independent and complete in the meaning it intends, it is difficult to judge whether they were revealed all at once or accompanied the rest of the chapter's verses in their revelation, as there is no evidence for this in their contents. As for what has been narrated regarding the occasions of revelation (أسباب النزول), some of the most significant reports will be mentioned in the "Discussion of Traditions."

The same applies to these three verses under discussion; the third verse is independent in its meaning, and the first verse is independent of it, even if it is not devoid of a certain type of suitability (مناسبة). There is some connection between them in that one of the instances of "vain oaths" (لغو اليمين) is when an oath pertains to the prohibition of certain wholesome things (طيبات) that Allah the Exalted has made lawful. Perhaps this was the motivation for those from whom it is narrated in the occasions of revelation that they mentioned the revelation of all these verses together regarding vain oaths.

This is the state of the first verse in relation to the third. As for the second verse, it is as if it is a completion of the first, as evidenced to some extent by its conclusion—namely, His saying, the Exalted: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي أَنْتُمْ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ** (and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith). Indeed, its beginning also suggests this, as it contains a conjunction and a command to eat of the lawful and wholesome things which the first verse forbids prohibiting and avoiding. Thus, the two verses harmonize in meaning and unite in ruling, possessing a single context (سياق).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ** (O you who have faith! Do not prohibit the good things that Allah has made lawful to you). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The prohibited (الحرام) is that which is forbidden, either by divine decree (تسخير إلهي), by coercive prevention (منع قهري), or by prevention from the perspective of reason (العقل), the law (الشرع), or from one whose command is followed." This concludes the relevant portion.

He also said: "The root of lawfulness (الحل) is the untying of a knot (حل العقدة); from this is His saying, Mighty and Majestic: **وَاحْلُلْ عُقْدَةً مِنْ لِسانِي** (and untie the knot from my tongue). And 'I settled' (حللت) means 'I descended'; its origin is from 'untying the loads' (حل الأحمال) upon descending, then its usage was stripped to mean simply descending. Thus it is said: 'he settled' (حل حلولا) and 'another settled him' (أحله غيره). Allah, Mighty and Majestic, said: **أَوْ تَحُلُّ قَرِيباً مِنْ دارِهِمْ** (or it alights near their homes) and **وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ** (and settled their people in the abode of ruin). It is also said: 'the debt became due' (حل الدين), meaning its payment became obligatory. A *hullah* (الحلة) refers to a group that has settled, and a *hayy halal* (حي حلال) is similar. A *mahalla* (المحلة) is the place of settling. From 'untying the knot' (حل العقدة), their saying 'the thing became lawful' (حل الشيء حلا) was metaphorically derived."

Allah the Exalted said: **وَكُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ حَلالاً طَيِّباً** (Eat of what Allah has provided you as lawful and good) and He the Exalted said: **هذا حَلالٌ وَهذا حَرامٌ** (This is lawful and this is forbidden). End quote.

It appears that the opposition of lawfulness (الحل) is prohibition (الحرمة), just as the opposition between being out of *ihram* (الحل) and being in *ihram* (الحرم أو الإحرام) arises from the conceptualization of a "knot" (العقد) in the restriction—which is the meaning of prohibition and its like—and then its opposition with "untying" (الحل), which is metaphorically used for the meaning of permissibility (الجواز) and license (الإباحة). These two terms—lawfulness and prohibition—were linguistic realities (حقائق عرفية) before Islam, rather than being purely legal (شرعية) or technical religious terms (متشرعة).

The verse—namely His saying: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا** (O you who have faith! Do not prohibit...) etc.—forbids the believers from prohibiting what Allah has made lawful for them. Prohibiting what Allah has made lawful means making it forbidden (حرام) just as Allah the Exalted made it lawful (حلال). This occurs either by legislating in opposition to His legislation, or by prevention (المنع) or abstention (الامتناع) such that one leaves something lawful by refusing to perform it, or by preventing oneself or others from it. All of this constitutes prohibition, restriction, and a contention with Allah, the Glorified, in His sovereignty (سلطانه) and an encroachment (اعتداء) upon Him that contradicts faith in Allah and His signs. For this reason, the prohibition began with the address: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (O you who have faith!), for the meaning is: Do not prohibit what Allah has made lawful for you while you have believed in Him and submitted to His command.

This is also supported by His saying at the end of the following verse: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي أَنْتُمْ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ** (and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith).

The addition of His saying: **طَيِّباتِ** (good things) to His saying: **ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ** (what Allah has made lawful to you)—even though the sentence would be complete without it—is to point toward the completion of the reason for the prohibition. For the believers' prohibition of what Allah has made lawful for them, while being an encroachment upon Allah's sovereignty and a violation of their faith in Him and submission to His command, is also a departure from the judgment of the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being*. This is because the primordial nature finds these lawful things wholesome (تستطيب) without any loathsomeness (استخباث). Allah, the Glorified, has informed of this in His description of His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and the law (الشريعة) he brought, where He said: **الَّذِينَ يَتَّبِعُونَ الرَّسُولَ النَّبِيَّ الْأُمِّيَّ الَّذِي يَجِدُونَهُ مَكْتُوباً عِنْدَهُمْ فِي التَّوْراةِ وَالْإِنْجِيلِ يَأْمُرُهمْ بِالْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهاهُمْ عَنِ الْمُنْكَرِ وَيُحِلُّ لَهُمُ الطَّيِّباتِ وَيُحَرِّمُ عَلَيْهِمُ الْخَبائِثَ وَيَضَعُ عَنْهُمْ إِصْرَهُمْ وَالْأَغْلالَ الَّتِي كانَتْ عَلَيْهِمْ فَالَّذِينَ آمَنُوا بِهِ وَعَزَّرُوهُ وَنَصَرُوهُ وَاتَّبَعُوا النُّورَ الَّذِي أُنْزِلَ مَعَهُ أُولئِكَ هُمُ الْمُفْلِحُونَ** (—those who follow the Apostle, the uninstructed prophet, whose mention they find written with them in the Torah and the Evangel, who bids them to do what is right and forbids them from what is wrong, makes lawful to them all the good things and forbids them from all vicious things, and relieves them of their burdens and the shackles that were upon them —those who believe in him, honour him, and help him and follow the light that has been sent down with him, they are the felicitous) (7:157).

By what we have explained, it is supported, firstly: that the meaning of prohibiting the good things Allah has made lawful is the obligation (الإلزام) and commitment (الالتزام) to abandon lawful things.

**Secondly:** that the meaning of lawfulness (الحل) is the opposite of prohibition (الحرمة), and it encompasses the permissible (المباحات), the recommended (المستحبات), and even the obligatory (الواجبات), in fulfillment of the requirement of opposition.

**Thirdly:** that the addition of "good things" (الطيبات) to "what Allah has made lawful" in His saying: **طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ** is an explanatory addition (إضافة بيانية).

**Fourthly:** that the meaning of encroachment (الاعتداء) in His saying: **وَلا تَعْتَدُوا** (and do not transgress) is encroachment upon Allah, the Glorified, in His legislative sovereignty (سلطانه التشريعي), or transgressing the limits (حدود) of Allah by divesting oneself of obedience to Him and submission to Him, and prohibiting what He has made lawful. As He the Exalted said at the end of the verse of divorce: **تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ فَلا تَعْتَدُوها وَمَنْ يَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَ اللهِ فَأُولئِكَ هُمُ الظَّالِمُونَ** (These are the bounds of Allah, so do not transgress them, and whoever transgresses the bounds of Allah —it is they who are the wrongdoers) (2:229). And His saying at the end of the verses of inheritance: **تِلْكَ حُدُودُ اللهِ وَمَنْ يُطِعِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ جَنَّاتٍ تَجْرِي مِنْ تَحْتِهَا الْأَنْهارُ خالِدِينَ فِيها وَذلِكَ الْفَوْزُ الْعَظِيمُ * وَمَنْ يَعْصِ اللهَ وَرَسُولَهُ وَيَتَعَدَّ حُدُودَهُ يُدْخِلْهُ ناراً خالِداً فِيها وَلَهُ عَذابٌ مُهِينٌ** (These are Allah’s bounds, and whoever obeys Allah and His Apostle, He shall admit him into gardens with conduits running thru them, to remain in them [forever]. That is the great triumph. But whoever disobeys Allah and His Apostle and transgresses His bounds, He shall make him enter a Fire, to remain in it [forever], and there is a humiliating punishment for him) (4:13-14). These verses—as you see—consider steadfastness and commitment to what Allah has legislated as a praised obedience to Him and His Messenger, while departing from submission, commitment, and compliance is considered a censured encroachment and transgression of Allah's limits, for which one is punished.

The sum of the verse's meaning is the prohibition of making what Allah has made lawful forbidden by avoiding it and refraining from approaching it. For this contradicts faith in Allah and His signs and opposes the fact that these lawful things are wholesome (طيبات) with no viciousness (خباثة) in them that would necessitate avoidance. It is an encroachment, and Allah does not love the encroachers.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلا تَعْتَدُوا إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُحِبُّ الْمُعْتَدِينَ** (and do not transgress. Indeed Allah does not like the transgressors). You have already seen that the apparent context (ظاهر السياق) indicates that the intended encroachment is the aforementioned prohibition in the preceding sentence. Thus, His saying: **وَلا تَعْتَدُوا** serves as an emphasis (تأكيد) for His saying: **لا تُحَرِّمُوا** (Do not prohibit...) etc.

As for what some have mentioned: that the intended encroachment is exceeding the limit of moderation in lawful things by throwing oneself into their enjoyment and seeking pleasure from them, as opposed to abandoning and avoiding their consumption out of asceticism (تقشف) and monkery (ترهب). Thus, the meaning of the verse would be: Do not prohibit for yourselves what Allah has made lawful for you of the pleasurable good things by intentionally abandoning their enjoyment out of devotion (تنسك) and seeking proximity to Him, the Exalted; and do not encroach by exceeding the limit of moderation into extravagance (إسراف) and excess (إفراط) that is harmful to your bodies or souls.

Or that the intended encroachment is bypassing the lawful good things for the prohibited vicious things (الخبائث). The meaning would then return to: do not avoid the lawful things and do not commit the prohibited things. In other words: do not prohibit what Allah has made lawful for you, and do not make lawful what Allah has prohibited for you.

Each of these two meanings, while correct in itself and supported by the Book without any doubt, does not align with the verse according to its apparent context and the context of the following verse. Not every correct meaning can be imposed upon every word, regardless of how it is phrased or where it occurs.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَكُلُوا مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ حَلالاً طَيِّباً** (Eat of what Allah has provided you as lawful and good) until the end of the verse. The apparent conjunction—namely the connection of His saying: **وَكُلُوا** (And eat) to His saying: **لا تُحَرِّمُوا** (Do not prohibit)—is that the meaning of this verse is in the position of repetition and emphasis for the content of the previous verse. This is supported by the context of the beginning of the verse, as it contains His saying: **حَلالاً طَيِّباً** (lawful and good), which parallels His saying in the previous verse: **طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ** (the good things that Allah has made lawful). Likewise, its conclusion parallels the conclusion of the previous verse, in the correspondence between His saying here: **وَاتَّقُوا اللهَ الَّذِي أَنْتُمْ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ** (and be wary of Allah in whom you have faith) and His saying in the previous verse: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (O you who have faith!). This has already been explained.

Accordingly, His saying: **كُلُوا** (Eat...) etc., is of the category of a command following a prohibition (الأمر عقيب الحظر). The specification of His saying: **كُلُوا** (Eat) after the generalization of His saying: **لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ** (Do not prohibit the good things...) etc., is either a specification in terms of wording only—where "eating" refers to any form of disposal (التصرف) of the wholesome blessings Allah has provided, whether by eating in the sense of nourishment or by other modes of disposal. It has been mentioned repeatedly that the use of "eating" (الأكل) to mean general disposal is a widespread and common usage.

Or it is possible that "eating" is intended in its literal sense, and the occasion of revelation for the two verses was that some believers during the time of revelation prohibited certain wholesome foods for themselves. Thus, the two verses were revealed to forbid that. The prohibition in the first verse was made general to include eating and other things to provide a universal rule (القاعدة الكلية), since the criterion (ملاك) for the prohibition applies equally to lawful foods and other things.

His saying: **مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ** (of what Allah has provided you), according to what we have inferred from the meaning of the two verses, is the object (مفعول) of His saying: **كُلُوا** (Eat), and His saying: **حَلالاً طَيِّباً** (lawful and good) are circumstantial qualifiers (حالين) for the relative pronoun (الموصول). In this way, the two verses harmonize. It has also been said that His saying: **حَلالاً طَيِّباً** is the object of His saying: **كُلُوا**, and His saying: **مِمَّا رَزَقَكُمُ اللهُ** is connected to His saying: **كُلُوا** or is a circumstantial qualifier for "lawful" (الحلال) that was placed before it because it is indefinite (نكرة), or that His saying: **حَلالاً** is an adjective for an omitted verbal noun (مصدر محذوف), the estimation being: "a lawful and good provision" (رزقا حلالا طيبا), among other views.

Some have used His saying: **حَلالاً** (lawful) as evidence that "provision" (الرزق) includes both the lawful and the prohibited; otherwise, the qualifier would be redundant.

The response is that it is not a restrictive qualifier (قيد احترازي) to exclude provision that is not lawful or good, but rather an explanatory qualifier (قيد توضيحي) equal to what it qualifies. The point in mentioning it is to clarify that its being lawful and good leaves no excuse for anyone to avoid or refrain from it, as previously mentioned. The discussion on the meaning of "provision" (الرزق) has already passed under the commentary of verse 27 of Surah Al-Imran in the third volume of this book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ** (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths, but He will take you to task for the oaths you have pledged). "Vain" (اللغو) refers to that which results in no effect among actions. "Oaths" (الأيمان) is the plural of *yamin* (يمين), which is a vow or swearing. Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "The *yamin* in swearing is metaphorically derived from the 'hand' (اليد), considering what is done by those making a covenant or alliance and others. Allah the Exalted said: **أَمْ لَكُمْ أَيْمانٌ عَلَيْنا بالِغَةٌ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ** (Or do you have any oaths [binding] on Us, reaching until the Day of Resurrection?), **وَأَقْسَمُوا بِاللهِ جَهْدَ أَيْمانِهِمْ** (And they swear by Allah with their most solemn oaths), and **لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ** (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths)." End quote. "Pledging" (التعقيد) is an intensive form of "tying" (العقد); it has also been recited as *‘aqadtum* (عقدتم) without intensification. His saying: **فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ** (in your oaths) is connected to His saying: **لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ** (He will not take you to task) or to His saying: **بِاللَّغْوِ** (for what is vain), which is closer.

The opposition between His saying: **بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ** (for what is vain in your oaths) and His saying: **بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ** (for the oaths you have pledged) indicates that "vain" in oaths refers to that which the swearer does not intend to bind (لا يعقد عليه), but rather it flows from his tongue out of habit or otherwise—such as their saying, especially in buying and selling: "No, by Allah" and "Yes, by Allah"—as opposed to what is bound by a pledge (عقد) of commitment to perform or abandon an act, such as one saying: "By Allah, I shall certainly do such-and-such," or "By Allah, I shall certainly leave such-and-such."

This is the apparent meaning of the verse. This does not contradict the fact that the law (شرعا) may consider the saying of one: "By Allah, I shall certainly do such-and-such a prohibited act," or "By Allah, I shall certainly leave such-and-such an obligation," as being among vain oaths, because the Legislator has nullified the oath in that which has no merit (رجحان). This is an inclusion by way of the Sunnah, and it is not necessary for the Quran to point to every specific detail established by the Sunnah.

As for His saying: **وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ** (but He will take you to task for the oaths you have pledged), it only includes oaths that are legally valid (الممضاة شرعا) because of His saying at the end of the verse: **وَاحْفَظُوا أَيْمانَكُمْ** (and keep your oaths). There is no escape from its inclusion of these oaths according to the generality of its wording, and there is no meaning in commanding the keeping of oaths that Allah, the Glorified, has nullified. Thus, it is determined that "vain" among oaths in the verse is that which contains no pledge (عقد), and that which is pledged is the valid oath.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَكَفَّارَتُهُ إِطْعامُ عَشَرَةِ مَساكِينَ** (its atonement is the feeding of ten needy persons) until His saying: **أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ** (or the freeing of a slave). Atonement (الكفارة) is the act by which the evil of a sin is covered in some way, from *kufr* (الكفر) meaning "covering." Allah the Exalted said: **نُكَفِّرْ عَنْكُمْ سَيِّئاتِكُمْ** (We will absolve you of your misdeeds) (4:31). Al-Raghib said: "Atonement (الكفارة) is that which covers the sin, and from it is the atonement for an oath." End quote.

His saying: **فَكَفَّارَتُهُ** (its atonement) is a derivation (تفريع) based on the oath, considering an implied premise such as: "If you break the oath (حنثتم), then its atonement is such-and-such." This is because the word "atonement" indicates a sin to which the atonement relates, and this sin is not the oath itself. If it were so, He would not have mentioned at the end of the verse: **وَاحْفَظُوا أَيْمانَكُمْ** (and keep your oaths), as there is no meaning in keeping that which contains a sin. Thus, the atonement relates to the breaking of the oath (حنث اليمين), not to the oath itself.

From this, it becomes clear that the "taking to task" (المؤاخذة) mentioned in His saying: **وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ** is the taking to task for breaking the oath, not for the act of making it. It was only attributed to the oath because its object—namely the breaking—relates to it. Thus, His saying: **فَكَفَّارَتُهُ** is derived from the implied breaking because His saying: **يُؤاخِذُكُمُ** (He will take you to task...) etc., indicates it. A similar explanation applies to His saying: **ذلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمانِكُمْ إِذا حَلَفْتُمْ** (That is the atonement for your oaths when you swear), the estimation being: "when you swear and then break it."

His saying: **إِطْعامُ عَشَرَةِ مَساكِينَ مِنْ أَوْسَطِ ما تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ** (the feeding of ten needy persons with the average of what you feed your families, or their clothing, or the freeing of a slave) are three options. The use of "or" (الترديد) indicates the determination of one of them upon breaking the oath without combining them. His saying afterward: **فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ ثَلاثَةِ أَيَّامٍ** (but whoever cannot afford [that], then a three-day fast) indicates that the aforementioned options are elective (تخييرية) without the necessity of observing the order in which they were mentioned. Otherwise, the derivation in His saying: **فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ** (but whoever cannot afford...) etc., would be redundant, and the context would have required saying: "or a three-day fast."

In the verse, there are many subsidiary discussions whose reference is the science of jurisprudence (علم الفقه).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ذلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمانِكُمْ إِذا حَلَفْتُمْ** (That is the atonement for your oaths when you swear). It was previously mentioned that the speech is in the estimation of: "when you swear and break it." In His saying: **ذلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمانِكُمْ** and likewise in His saying: **كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللهُ لَكُمْ** (Thus does Allah clarify for you), there is a type of transition (التفات) and return from addressing the believers to addressing the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Perhaps the point in this is that both sentences are part of the divine clarification for the people, which is only through the mediation of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). Thus, it preserves his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) station in clarifying what was revealed to him for the people, as He the Exalted said: **وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ** (We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify for the people what has been sent down to them) (16:44).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **كَذلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ اللهُ لَكُمْ آياتِهِ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَشْكُرُونَ** (Thus does Allah clarify His signs for you so that you may give thanks), meaning He clarifies His rulings for you through His Prophet so that you may thank Him by learning them and acting upon them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Tafsir al-Qummi*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ** (the verse): He said: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from some of his men, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "This verse was revealed concerning the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), Bilal, and ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un. As for the Commander of the Faithful (peace be upon him), he swore that he would never sleep at night. As for Bilal, he swore that he would never break his fast during the day. As for ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un, he swore that he would never engage in sexual intercourse."

The wife of ‘Uthman entered upon ‘Aishah, and she was a beautiful woman. ‘Aishah said: "Why do I see you neglected?" She replied: "For whom should I adorn myself? By Allah, my husband has not approached me for such-and-such a time, for he has become a monk, wears haircloth (المسوح), and has renounced the world."

When the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) entered, ‘Aishah informed him of that. He went out and called for a congregational prayer. The people gathered, and he ascended the pulpit, praised Allah, and extolled Him, then said: "What is the matter with certain people who prohibit for themselves the good things? Behold, I sleep at night, I marry, and I break my fast during the day. Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me."

These men stood up and said: "O Messenger of Allah, we have sworn to that." So Allah revealed to him: **لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ فَكَفَّارَتُهُ إِطْعامُ عَشَرَةِ مَساكِينَ مِنْ أَوْسَطِ ما تُطْعِمُونَ أَهْلِيكُمْ أَوْ كِسْوَتُهُمْ أَوْ تَحْرِيرُ رَقَبَةٍ فَمَنْ لَمْ يَجِدْ فَصِيامُ ثَلاثَةِ أَيَّامٍ ذلِكَ كَفَّارَةُ أَيْمانِكُمْ إِذا حَلَفْتُمْ** (Allah will not take you to task for what is vain in your oaths, but He will take you to task for the oaths you have pledged. Its atonement is the feeding of ten needy persons with the average of what you feed your families, or their clothing, or the freeing of a slave. But whoever cannot afford [that], then a three-day fast. That is the atonement for your oaths when you swear).

**I say:** There is some obscurity in the application of His saying, the Exalted: **لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ وَلكِنْ يُؤاخِذُكُمْ بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ** (the verse) to their swearing, as some discussion has already passed. Al-Tabrisi narrated the story in *Majma‘ al-Bayan* from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) without mentioning the conclusion, so let it be reflected upon.

In *al-Ihtijaj*, from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali (peace be upon him) in a tradition: that he said to Mu‘awiyah and his companions: "I adjure you by Allah—do you not know that ‘Ali was the first of the companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) to prohibit desires for himself? So Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ**."

In *al-Majma‘*, regarding the verse: The commentators said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) sat one day and reminded the people, describing the Resurrection. The people became soft-hearted and wept. Ten of the companions gathered in the house of ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un al-Jumahi; they were ‘Ali, Abu Bakr, ‘Abdullah ibn Mas‘ud, Abu Dharr al-Ghifari, Salim the client of Abu Hudhayfah, ‘Abdullah ibn ‘Umar, al-Miqdad ibn al-Aswad al-Kindi, Salman al-Farisi, and Ma‘qil ibn Muqarrin. They agreed to fast during the day, stand in prayer at night, not sleep on beds, not eat meat or fat, not approach women or perfume, wear haircloth, reject the world, and travel through the land. Some of them even intended to castrate themselves.

News of this reached the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so he went to the house of ‘Uthman but did not find him. He said to his wife, Umm Hakim bint Abi Umayyah—her name was Hawla’ and she was a perfumer: "Is what has reached me about your husband and his companions true?" She disliked lying to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and disliked exposing her husband, so she said: "O Messenger of Allah, if ‘Uthman informed you, then he has told you the truth." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) departed, and when ‘Uthman entered, she informed him of that.

He and his companions came to the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), and the Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to them: "Have I not been informed that you agreed on such-and-such?" They said: "Yes, O Messenger of Allah, and we intended nothing but good." The Messenger of Allah (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said: "I was not commanded with that." Then he said: "Indeed, your souls have a right over you; so fast and break your fast, stand in prayer and sleep. For I stand and sleep, I fast and break my fast, I eat meat and fat, and I approach women. Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me."

Then he gathered the people and addressed them, saying: "What is the matter with certain people who have prohibited women, food, perfume, sleep, and the desires of the world? Behold, I do not command you to be priests or monks, for there is no abandoning of meat in my religion, nor of women, nor the taking of hermitages. Indeed, the travel (سياحة) of my community is fasting, and their monkery (رهبانية) is Jihad. Worship Allah and do not associate anything with Him, perform the Hajj and the ‘Umrah, establish the prayer, pay the Zakat, fast Ramadan, and be upright, and you will be made upright. For those before you only perished through severity (التشديد); they were severe upon themselves, so Allah was severe upon them. Those are their remnants in the monasteries and hermitages." Then Allah revealed the verse.

**I say:** It appears by referring to the narrations of the [Sunni] group that this narration is merely a summary of the narrations reported on this subject, which are very numerous. He has presented it by combining their scattered contents, inserting some into others, and casting them as a single narration.

As for these narrations themselves, despite their abundance, the names of these companions do not all appear together in any one of them. Rather, the most comprehensive of them in wording mentions these companions with the phrase "‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un and his companions"; in some, "people from the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله)"; and in others, "men from the companions of the Prophet (ص)."

Likewise, what occurred in this narration regarding the speech of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and its details are found as separate sentences in the narrations. Similarly, regarding what they pledged and intended to abandon, the narrations do not explicitly state that all of them agreed on all of those things. Rather, some narrations state their differences in what they intended or pledged, as in the *Sahih* of al-Bukhari and Muslim from ‘Aishah: "People from the companions of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) asked the wives of the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) about his actions in private. Some of them said: 'I will not eat meat'; some said: 'I will not marry women'; and some said: 'I will not sleep on a bed.' This reached the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله), so he said: 'What is the matter with certain people, one of whom says such-and-such? But I fast and break my fast, I sleep and I stand, I eat meat, and I marry women. Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me.'"

Perhaps the meaning of the statement in the narration "and they agreed to fast during the day..." etc., is that the group as a whole agreed on the set of actions, not that every single one of them resolved upon all of them. Although the narrations differ in their contents—and among them are the weak, the disconnected (المرسل), and the reliable—reflection upon all of them leads to the confidence that a group of the companions resolved upon this type of asceticism and devotion, and that ‘Ali (peace be upon him) and ‘Uthman ibn Maz‘un were among them, and that the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) said to them: "Whoever turns away from my Sunnah is not of me." Allah knows best. You should refer to the traditional commentaries such as the *Tafsir* of al-Tabari, *al-Durr al-Manthur*, *Fath al-Qadir*, and their like.

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Al-Tirmidhi (who graded it fair), Ibn Jarir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Ibn ‘Adi in *al-Kamil*, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas: that a man came to the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and said: "O Messenger of Allah, when I eat meat, I become aroused for women and my desire takes hold of me, so I have prohibited meat for myself." Then was revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ**.

In the same source: Ibn Jarir and Ibn Abi Hatim narrated from Zayd ibn Aslam: that a guest from his family visited ‘Abdullah ibn Rawahah while he was with the Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله). When he returned to his family, he found they had not fed their guest, waiting for him. He said to his wife: "You have kept my guest for my sake; it is prohibited (حرام) for me." His wife said: "It is prohibited for me." The guest said: "It is prohibited for me." When he saw that, he placed his hand and said: "Eat in the name of Allah." Then he went to the Prophet (ص) and informed him. The Prophet (ص) said: "You have done right." Then Allah revealed: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ**.

**I say:** It is possible that the two reasons mentioned in the last two narrations are from the application (تطبيق) of the narrators, which is common in narrations of the occasions of revelation. It is also possible for a verse to have numerous occasions for its revelation.

In *Tafsir al-‘Ayyashi*, from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, who said: I asked him about a man who said to his wife: "[You are] divorced," or "my slaves are free if I drink anything prohibited or lawful." He said: "As for the prohibited, he should not approach it whether he swore or not. As for the lawful, he should not leave it, for it is not for him to prohibit what Allah has made lawful, because Allah says: **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ**. Thus, there is nothing upon him regarding his oath concerning the lawful."

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of narration from Mas‘adah ibn Sadaqah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: I heard him saying regarding the saying of Allah, Mighty and Majestic: **لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ** — He said: "The 'vain' (اللغو) is the saying of a man: 'No, by Allah' and 'Yes, by Allah,' without pledging (يعقد) anything."

**I say:** Al-‘Ayyashi narrated the same in his *Tafsir* from ‘Abdullah ibn Sinan, and from Muhammad ibn Muslim similarly, which adds: "and he does not pledge it."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Ibn Jarir narrated from Ibn ‘Abbas, who said: When **يا أَيُّهَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا لا تُحَرِّمُوا طَيِّباتِ ما أَحَلَّ اللهُ لَكُمْ** was revealed concerning the people who prohibited women and meat for themselves, they said: "O Messenger of Allah, what shall we do with our oaths that we swore?" So Allah revealed: **لا يُؤاخِذُكُمُ اللهُ بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ**.

**I say:** This narration resembles the conclusion of the first narration we cited at the beginning of the discussion, except that it does not align with the apparent meaning of the verse. For swearing to abandon an obligation or a permissible act is not devoid of a pledge (عقد) upon it, and the verse has opposed His saying: **بِاللَّغْوِ فِي أَيْمانِكُمْ** with His saying: **بِما عَقَّدْتُمُ الْأَيْمانَ**. This indicates that the vain oath is that which has no pledge. This apparent meaning only agrees with the narration interpreting the vain oath as the saying of one: "No, by Allah" and "Yes, by Allah," without pledging anything. As for the oath nullified by the law, it contains a pledge upon what was sworn; thus, its nullification must be based on the Sunnah rather than the Book.

Furthermore, the context of the verse is the strongest evidence that it was set forth to clarify the atonement for an oath and the command to keep it independently, not in a secondary manner (تطفل) as this interpretation would necessitate.
